label: Fiat dilution and the historical record
brief:
The historical record of state-managed currencies is unambiguous: every fiat regime in recorded history has been inflated, often catastrophically, eventually [1]. Rothbard catalogues the pattern from the Roman denarius (debased steadily from ~95% silver under Augustus to under 5% by the third century, contributing materially to the empire's monetary collapse) through medieval coin clipping, John Law's eighteenth-century paper experiment in France, the American Continentals (which gave the language the phrase 'not worth a Continental'), the Weimar mark, the Hungarian pengő (the worst hyperinflation in recorded history, with prices doubling every fifteen hours at peak), Zimbabwe, Venezuela, and dozens more [1].

The pattern is not random. It follows from the structure of the situation: any institution with the unilateral power to create money at zero marginal cost faces a constant temptation to use that power, and that temptation gets exercised under fiscal stress. Hülsmann argues this in *The Ethics of Money Production* — debasement is not a regrettable lapse in monetary policy, it is the predictable equilibrium of any monopoly issuer, because the *option* to debase is itself a fiscal asset that politicians cannot afford to leave unexercised forever [2].

The modern post-1971 experiment with floating fiat money is, on this reading, just the most recent iteration of a very old pattern. The dollar has lost roughly 95% of its purchasing power against a basket of consumer goods since the founding of the Federal Reserve in 1913, and the pace has accelerated since the closing of the gold window. The same is broadly true of every other major fiat currency over the same window [3]. None of this is to predict imminent collapse — fiat regimes can sustain themselves for decades or centuries; the Roman denarius took two hundred years to fully unwind. The claim is structural: the *direction* is monotonic, even if the speed varies.

What Bitcoin offers is the first credibly-bound monetary system in history that is not subject to this dynamic — because there is no issuer with the option to debase. The supply schedule is enforced by every node, and changing it would require coordinated convincing of the operators themselves, who have no fiscal incentive to inflate.

sources.label: Murray N. Rothbard — What Has Government Done to Our Money?
sources.excerpt: The history of money has been the history of government interference with that money. Every state-managed currency has eventually been inflated; not as an accident, but as the working-out of the underlying incentive structure.

sources.label: Jörg Guido Hülsmann — The Ethics of Money Production
sources.excerpt: Inflation is the inevitable companion of fiat money, because the option to inflate is itself a fiscal resource. No issuer with that option ultimately leaves it unused; the question is only the timing and the magnitude.

sources.label: Mises Institute Wire — Sound Money, Bitcoin, and the End of Fiat
sources.excerpt: The post-1971 dollar has lost roughly 95% of its purchasing power against a stable basket of goods. Other major fiat currencies show similar trajectories. This is not unusual; it is the historical default.
